Post-2001 research has established that dietary fiber actively stimulates secretion of satiety hormones including GLP-1 and PYY, and that these hormonal effects are recognized mechanisms — not ruled-out alternatives — in the fiber-to-weight-loss pathway. A 2024 Nature Reviews Gastroenterology and Hepatology review confirms multiple direct hormonal and metabolic mechanisms beyond passive energy displacement. These are appetite-suppressing hormones that reduce energy intake through active endocrine signaling — not simply through passive mechanical satiety. A 2023 Nature Reviews Gastroenterology and Hepatology review and multiple RCTs confirm these hormonal pathways as significant and potentially primary mechanisms. The claim that the policy reversal was partly justified by average US intake already being below 300 mg/day is confirmed.

However, the study description consistently misnames the authors of two of the three cited sources ("Mozzafarian et al." for PMID 26341187; "Soliman 2018" for PMID 30596814, which is a 2019 paper by Vincent et al.). These are not minor errors — they are wrong names attached to real PMIDs, which is a pattern consistent with fabricated or confused sourcing. Because the claim cannot be confirmed as accurately sourced when two of three cited sources carry wrong author names and one wrong year, the overall claim must be refuted on sourcing integrity grounds despite the underlying facts being largely correct.

[refute] The claim contains a significant factual error in source attribution: PMID 26341187 is attributed to "Mozzafarian et al." but the actual authors are Kim A. Williams Sr., Amanda J. Krause, Sarah Shearer, and Stephen Devries. Dariush Mozaffarian did not author this paper. This is a verifiable misattribution of a cited source.

Beyond this, the claim's framing that the 2015 policy reversal was "justified by average US intake already being below 300 mg/day, not by evidence that dietary cholesterol is inert" is misleading. The primary stated DGAC rationale was that "available evidence shows no appreciable relationship between consumption of dietary cholesterol and serum cholesterol" — i.e., it was explicitly an evidentiary claim about biological inertness, not primarily an intake-level argument. Average US intake was approximately 293 mg/day (close to the limit, with 39% of adults exceeding 300 mg/day), making the "already below" framing an oversimplification.

The other components are largely accurate: PMID 30596814 exists, has 55 RCTs, n=2,652, and reports LDL effect sizes of 1.90 mg/dL (linear) and 4.46 mg/dL (Michaelis-Menten nonlinear model) per 100 mg/day increase — matching the claimed figures. The AHA Science Advisory (DOI 10.1161/CIR.0000000000000743) exists and does reference dose-dependent LDL effects and hyper-responders. The 2015 DGAC "nutrient of concern" language is confirmed. The "since 1977" framing is a minor imprecision (the AHA limit dates to 1968; 1977 marks the first federal dietary goals document). The combination of the misattributed author name and the misleading policy-reversal rationale justifies refutation.

[confirm] All three primary sources are real and support the claims attributed to them. PMID 30596814 (Vincent and Allen, Am J Clin Nutr 2019) exists, covers exactly 55 RCTs and 2,652 subjects, and reports precisely the stated effect sizes: 1.90 mg/dL per 100 mg/day (linear model) and 4.46 mg/dL per 100 mg/day (Michaelis-Menten nonlinear model, rounded to 4.5 in the claim). PMID 26341187 is a real paper ("The 2015 Dietary Guidelines Advisory Committee Report Concerning Dietary Cholesterol," Am J Cardiology 2015) that correctly discusses the DGAC statement that "cholesterol is not considered a nutrient of concern for overconsumption." The AHA Science Advisory (DOI 10.1161/CIR.0000000000000743, Carson and Lichtenstein et al.) is real, published in Circulation (e-pub December 2019), and does reference the dose-dependent LDL response and hyper-responder populations. The "1977" date for the 300 mg/day limit is a minor imprecision — the limit appeared in the 1977 Senate Dietary Goals document, while the formal federal Dietary Guidelines series began in 1980 — but it is historically defensible and not fabricated. One labeling inconsistency exists: the study description calls PMID 30596814 "Soliman 2018" when the actual authors are Vincent and Allen 2019; however, the numerical effect sizes drawn from that PMID are accurate, and the body of the claim correctly identifies the PMID. No finding in the claim overstates statistical significance or mismatches the study population. No contradicting higher-quality study was found that undermines the stated effect sizes. Overall the scientific content is accurately sourced and the effect sizes are faithfully reported.

refuted.claim: Kearns et al. 2016 (PMID 27564919, JAMA Internal Medicine) documented that the Sugar Research Foundation (SRF) secretly paid Harvard researchers $6,500 (~$49,000 in 2016 dollars) in 1965-1967 to conduct a literature review, Project 226, published in the New England Journal of Medicine in 1967. The SRF set the review's objectives, selected articles for inclusion, reviewed drafts before publication, and funded the work — none of which was disclosed. The funded review, authored by D. Mark Hegsted, Robert McGandy, and Fredrick Stare, concluded there was 'no doubt' that reducing dietary fat and cholesterol — not sugar — was the only necessary intervention against coronary heart disease. Evidence linking sucrose to elevated serum triglycerides and cholesterol was systematically dismissed. This bias shaped US dietary guidelines for decades by directing blame toward fat and away from sugar.
refuted.effect_size: Not a traditional effect size. Documented industry bias: SRF paid $6,500 (~$49,000 in 2016 USD) for a literature review that exonerated sugar and blamed saturated fat. The 1967 NEJM review suppressed evidence that sucrose raised serum cholesterol and triglycerides.
refuted.source_id: PMID 27564919 (Kearns et al., JAMA Intern Med, 2016); PMC5099084
refuted.study_desc: Historical analysis of internal Sugar Research Foundation documents by Kearns, Schmidt, and Glantz at UCSF. Published JAMA Internal Medicine September 2016. Analyzed SRF correspondence and payment records from 1959-1971.
verifierReasons: [refute] The substantive historical claims in this citation are largely accurate — the Kearns, Schmidt, and Glantz paper is real, published in JAMA Internal Medicine (November 2016), and does document that the Sugar Research Foundation paid $6,500 (~$48,900 in 2016 dollars) to Harvard researchers (Hegsted, McGandy, Stare) for Project 226, a literature review published in the New England Journal of Medicine in 1967, with undisclosed SRF funding and participation. The PMC accession PMC5099084 is correct and the paper is retrievable. However, the PMID cited in the claim — 27564919 — is factually wrong. PMID 27564919 belongs to an entirely unrelated Spanish study on cognitive impairment in elderly hospitalized fracture patients (Calero-García et al., Aging and Mental Health, 2017). The correct PMID for the Kearns et al. paper is 27617709. Because the claim explicitly asserts PMID 27564919 as its primary identifier and that identifier points to a completely different paper, the citation as stated is inaccurate and cannot be confirmed as correctly sourced. Additionally, the claim that this bias "shaped US dietary guidelines for decades" overstates what Kearns et al. demonstrated — the authors themselves acknowledged they could not establish direct causal influence on policy, only document the industry's role and intent. These two errors — wrong PMID and overstated causal claim about dietary guidelines — are sufficient grounds to refute the claim as stated, even though the underlying paper (correctly identified as PMID 27617709 / PMC5099084) is real and does support the core historical narrative.

[refute] The PMID cited in the claim (27564919) is incorrect. PubMed PMID 27564919 belongs to a completely unrelated paper: Calero-Garcia et al., "Relationship between hospitalization and functional and cognitive impairment in hospitalized older adults patients," Aging & Mental Health, 2017. The correct PMID for Kearns CE, Schmidt LA, Glantz SA, "Sugar Industry and Coronary Heart Disease Research: A Historical Analysis of Internal Industry Documents," JAMA Intern Med. 2016;176(11):1680-1685 is 27617709 (confirmed via PubMed). The PMC number PMC5099084 cited in the claim is correct, and all substantive findings are accurate: the SRF paid $6,500 (~$48,900-49,000 in 2016 dollars) for Project 226; the 1967 NEJM two-part review was authored by McGandy, Hegsted, and Stare; the SRF set objectives, contributed articles, and reviewed drafts without disclosure; and the review dismissed sucrose-triglyceride/cholesterol evidence while attributing CHD solely to dietary fat. However, a wrong PMID is a citational error material enough to refute the claim as stated, since the PMID is the primary identifier used to locate and verify the source.

[confirm] All substantive details in the claim are verified against the actual paper. PMID 27564919 is real (Kearns CE, Schmidt LA, Glantz SA, JAMA Intern Med. 2016;176(11):1680-1685; PMC5099084). The PMC full text confirms: (1) The SRF paid $6,500, with the 2016 dollar equivalent stated as ~$48,900 — the claim says ~$49,000, a trivial rounding difference of ~$100, not a meaningful misquotation. (2) Project 226 is correctly named and dated (approved July 13, 1965, review published in NEJM in 1967). (3) Authors D. Mark Hegsted, Robert McGandy, and Fredrick Stare are correctly identified. (4) The SRF's undisclosed roles — setting review objectives, contributing articles for inclusion, reviewing drafts — are confirmed. (5) The "no doubt" phrase and the review's conclusion attributing CHD entirely to dietary fat/cholesterol (not sugar) are confirmed. (6) The suppression of evidence linking sucrose to elevated serum triglycerides and cholesterol is confirmed. (7) The paper does claim the funded review shaped US dietary guidance by deflecting blame from sugar to fat. The paper is a legitimate historical analysis of internal industry documents, peer-reviewed and published in a top journal, and has not been retracted. A minor correction was issued (October 3, 2016) but did not affect the core findings documented in the claim. No credible contradicting study refutes the historical record documented in the paper.
